infobox.value: is a unique or [novel](/wiki/Novelty_(patent) "Novelty (patent)") [device](/wiki/Machine "Machine"), [method](/wiki/Method_(patent) "Method (patent)"), composition, idea, or process. An invention may be an improvement upon a machine, product, or process for increasing efficiency or lowering cost. It may also be an entirely new concept. If an idea is unique enough either as a stand-alone invention or as a significant improvement over the work of others, it can be patented. A [patent](/wiki/Patent "Patent"), if granted, gives the inventor a proprietary interest in the patent over a specific period of time, which can be licensed for financial gain.

creates or discovers an invention. The word *inventor* comes from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") verb *invenire*, *invent-*, to find.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) Although inventing is closely associated with science and engineering, inventors are not necessarily engineers or scientists.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Due to advances in [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence"), the term "inventor" no longer exclusively applies to an occupation.[[4]](#cite_note-Hornby-4)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Novel device, material or technical process

"Invented" and "Inventor" redirect here. For other uses, see [Invention (disambiguation)](/wiki/Invention_(disambiguation) "Invention (disambiguation)") and [Inventor (disambiguation)](/wiki/Inventor_(disambiguation) "Inventor (disambiguation)").

[![Cover of Science and Invention Magazine](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Science_and_Invention_Nov_1928_Cover_2.jpg/250px-Science_and_Invention_Nov_1928_Cover_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Science_and_Invention_Nov_1928_Cover_2.jpg)

'BUILD YOUR OWN TELEVISION RECEIVER.' *[Science and Invention](/wiki/Electrical_Experimenter "Electrical Experimenter")* magazine cover, November 1928

An **invention** is a unique or [novel](/wiki/Novelty_(patent) "Novelty (patent)") [device](/wiki/Machine "Machine"), [method](/wiki/Method_(patent) "Method (patent)"), composition, idea, or process. An invention may be an improvement upon a machine, product, or process for increasing efficiency or lowering cost. It may also be an entirely new concept. If an idea is unique enough either as a stand-alone invention or as a significant improvement over the work of others, it can be patented. A [patent](/wiki/Patent "Patent"), if granted, gives the inventor a proprietary interest in the patent over a specific period of time, which can be licensed for financial gain.

An **inventor** creates or discovers an invention. The word *inventor* comes from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") verb *invenire*, *invent-*, to find.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) Although inventing is closely associated with science and engineering, inventors are not necessarily engineers or scientists.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Due to advances in [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence"), the term "inventor" no longer exclusively applies to an occupation.[[4]](#cite_note-Hornby-4)

Some inventions can be patented. The system of [patents](/wiki/Patent "Patent") was established to encourage inventors by granting limited-term, limited [monopoly](/wiki/Monopoly "Monopoly") on inventions determined to be sufficiently [novel](/wiki/Novelty_(patent) "Novelty (patent)"), [non-obvious](/wiki/Inventive_step_and_non-obviousness "Inventive step and non-obviousness"), and [useful](/wiki/Utility_(patentability_requirement) "Utility (patentability requirement)") or has [industrial applicability](/wiki/Industrial_applicability "Industrial applicability"). A patent is [jurisdictional](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction"), meaning that a patent only provides rights to the patent owner within the jurisdiction (Country or Countries) in which the patent was obtained. A patent provides the patent owner (who may or may not be an inventor) the right to exclude others from making, using, offering for sale, or selling an invention or importing it into the jurisdiction. The rules and requirements for patenting an invention vary by country and the process of obtaining a patent is often expensive.

Another meaning of invention is **[cultural invention](/wiki/Cultural_invention "Cultural invention")**, which is an [innovative](/wiki/Innovative "Innovative") set of useful [social behaviours](/wiki/Social_behaviour "Social behaviour") adopted by people and passed on to others.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The [Institute for Social Inventions](/wiki/Institute_for_Social_Inventions "Institute for Social Inventions") collected many such ideas in magazines and books.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Invention is also an important component of artistic and design [creativity](/wiki/Creativity "Creativity"). Inventions often extend the boundaries of human knowledge, experience or capability.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Invention&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The earliest inventions pre-date anatomically modern humans. Crude stone tools, such as the [Oldowan](/wiki/Oldowan "Oldowan") tools, date back around 2.9 million years. Other inventions of the Lower Paleolithic era are rafts[[7]](#cite_note-Plakias2010-7) and cooking (which dates back to [Homo habilis](/wiki/Homo_habilis "Homo habilis")).

Some of the first human inventions were bedding,[[8]](#cite_note-8) clothing,[[9]](#cite_note-9) and hafting[[10]](#cite_note-10). Other notable pre-historic inventions were agriculture, fish hooks, pottery (including bricks), bread, flutes, and [tally sticks](/wiki/Tally_sticks "Tally sticks").

The 21st century has seen several notable machine-generated inventions. For example, the 2006 [evolved antenna](/wiki/Evolved_antenna "Evolved antenna") from [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA"). [Genetic algorithms](/wiki/Genetic_algorithms "Genetic algorithms") are widely studied in computer science to develop software and the same approach has been with hardware since at least the 1990s.[[11]](#cite_note-11).

## Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Invention&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Types")]

Inventions are of three kinds: scientific-technological (including [medicine](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine")), sociopolitical (including economics and law), and humanistic, or cultural.

Scientific-technological inventions include railroads, [aviation](/wiki/Aviation "Aviation"), [vaccination](/wiki/Vaccination "Vaccination"), hybridization, [antibiotics](/wiki/Antibiotic "Antibiotic") [such as calpol], astronautics, [holography](/wiki/Holography "Holography"), the atomic bomb, computing, the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet"), and the smartphone.

Sociopolitical inventions comprise new laws, institutions, and procedures that change modes of social behavior and establish new forms of human interaction and organization. Examples include the British [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament "Parliament"), the US [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution "Constitution"), the Manchester (UK) General Union of Trades, [the Boy Scouts](/wiki/Boy_Scouts_of_America "Boy Scouts of America"), the [Red Cross](/wiki/Red_Cross "Red Cross"), the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games"), the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), and the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights"), as well as movements such as [socialism](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism"), [Zionism](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism"), [suffragism](/wiki/Suffragism "Suffragism"), [feminism](/wiki/Feminism "Feminism"), and animal-rights veganism.
Another example of an invention is the [walkman].
Humanistic inventions encompass culture in its entirety and are as transformative and important as any in the sciences, although people tend to take them for granted. In the domain of linguistics, for example, Hunminjeongeum[[12]](#cite_note-12) or many alphabets have been inventions, as are all [neologisms](/wiki/Neologism "Neologism") ([Shakespeare](/wiki/Shakespeare "Shakespeare") invented about 1,700 words). Literary inventions include the epic, [tragedy](/wiki/Tragedy "Tragedy"), comedy, the [novel](/wiki/Novel "Novel"), the [sonnet](/wiki/Sonnet "Sonnet"), the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), neoclassicism, [Romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism"), [Symbolism](/wiki/Symbolism_(arts) "Symbolism (arts)"), Aestheticism, [Socialist Realism](/wiki/Socialist_Realism "Socialist Realism"), [Surrealism](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism"), [postmodernism](/wiki/Postmodernism "Postmodernism"), and (according to Freud) [psychoanalysis](/wiki/Psychoanalysis "Psychoanalysis"). Among the inventions of artists and musicians are oil painting, printmaking, [photography](/wiki/Photography "Photography"), [cinema](/wiki/Film "Film"), musical tonality, atonality, [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), rock, [opera](/wiki/Opera "Opera"), and the symphony orchestra. Philosophers have invented logic (several times), [dialectics](/wiki/Dialectics "Dialectics"), idealism, materialism, [utopia](/wiki/Utopia "Utopia"), [anarchism](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism"), [semiotics](/wiki/Semiotics "Semiotics"), [phenomenology](/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy) "Phenomenology (philosophy)"), [behaviorism](/wiki/Behaviorism "Behaviorism"), [positivism](/wiki/Positivism "Positivism"), [pragmatism](/wiki/Pragmatism "Pragmatism"), and [deconstruction](/wiki/Deconstruction "Deconstruction"). Religious thinkers are responsible for such inventions as [monotheism](/wiki/Monotheism "Monotheism"), [pantheism](/wiki/Pantheism "Pantheism"), [Methodism](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"), [Mormonism](/wiki/Mormonism "Mormonism"), iconoclasm, [puritanism](/wiki/Puritanism "Puritanism"), [deism](/wiki/Deism "Deism"), secularism, ecumenism, and the [Baháʼí Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith"). Some of these disciplines, genres, and trends may seem to have existed eternally or to have emerged spontaneously of their own accord, but most of them have had inventors.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Process

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Invention&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Process")]

### Practical means

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Invention&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Practical means")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Alessandro_Volta.jpeg/250px-Alessandro_Volta.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Alessandro_Volta.jpeg)

[Alessandro Volta](/wiki/Alessandro_Volta "Alessandro Volta") with the first [electrical battery](/wiki/Electrical_battery "Electrical battery"). Volta is recognized as an influential inventor.

Ideas for an invention may be developed on paper or on a computer, by writing or drawing, by [trial and error](/wiki/Trial_and_error "Trial and error"), by making models, by [experimenting](/wiki/Experiment "Experiment"), by testing and/or by making the invention in its whole form. [Brainstorming](/wiki/Brainstorming "Brainstorming") also can spark new ideas for an invention. Collaborative creative processes are frequently used by engineers, designers, architects and scientists. Co-inventors are frequently named on patents.

In addition, many inventors keep [records](/wiki/Inventor%27s_notebook "Inventor's notebook") of their working process – [notebooks](/wiki/Inventor%27s_notebook "Inventor's notebook"), photos, etc., including [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci"), [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei"), [Evangelista Torricelli](/wiki/Evangelista_Torricelli "Evangelista Torricelli"), [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") and [Albert Einstein](/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein").[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In the process of developing an invention, the initial idea may change. The invention may become simpler, more practical, it may expand, or it may even *morph* into something totally different. Working on one invention can lead to others too.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

History shows that turning the concept of an invention into a working device is not always swift or direct. Inventions may also become more useful after time passes and other changes occur. For example, the [parachute](/wiki/Parachute "Parachute") became more useful once powered [flight](/wiki/Flight "Flight") was a reality.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Conceptual means

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Invention&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Conceptual means")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Edison_and_phonograph_edit2.jpg/250px-Edison_and_phonograph_edit2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edison_and_phonograph_edit2.jpg)

[Thomas Edison](/wiki/Thomas_Edison "Thomas Edison") with [phonograph](/wiki/Phonograph "Phonograph"). Edison was one of the most prolific inventors in history, holding [1,093 U.S. patents in his name](/wiki/List_of_Edison_patents "List of Edison patents").

Invention is often a [creative process](/wiki/Creative_process "Creative process"). An open and curious mind allows an inventor to see beyond what is known. Seeing a new possibility, connection or relationship can spark an invention. Inventive thinking frequently involves combining concepts or elements from different realms that would not normally be put together. Sometimes inventors disregard the boundaries between distinctly separate territories or fields.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Several concepts may be considered when thinking about invention.

#### Play

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Invention&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Play")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Gutenberg-Presse_im_Gutenberg-Museum%2C_Mainz%2C_Deutschland_%2848988294536%29.jpg/250px-Gutenberg-Presse_im_Gutenberg-Museum%2C_Mainz%2C_Deutschland_%2848988294536%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gutenberg-Presse_im_Gutenberg-Museum,_Mainz,_Deutschland_(48988294536).jpg)

[Johannes Gutenberg](/wiki/Johannes_Gutenberg "Johannes Gutenberg")'s [printing press](/wiki/Gutenberg_press "Gutenberg press") was voted the most important invention of the second millennium.[[20]](#cite_note-Man_of_the_Millennium-20)

Play may lead to invention. Childhood curiosity, experimentation, and imagination can develop one's play instinct. Inventors feel the need to play with things that interest them, and to explore, and this internal drive brings about novel creations.[[21]](#cite_note-inventionatplay1-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Sometimes inventions and ideas may seem to arise spontaneously while [daydreaming](/wiki/Daydreaming "Daydreaming"), especially when the mind is free from its usual concerns.[[23]](#cite_note-23) For example, both J. K. Rowling (the creator of [Harry Potter](/wiki/Harry_Potter "Harry Potter"))[[24]](#cite_note-24) and Frank Hornby (the inventor of [Meccano](/wiki/Meccano "Meccano"))[[25]](#cite_note-25) first had their ideas while on [train](/wiki/Train "Train") journeys.

In contrast, the successful aerospace engineer [Max Munk](/wiki/Max_Munk "Max Munk") advocated "aimful thinking".[[26]](#cite_note-26)

#### Re-envisioning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Invention&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Re-envisioning")]

To invent is to see anew. Inventors often envision a new idea, seeing it in their [mind's eye](/wiki/Mind%27s_eye "Mind's eye"). New ideas can arise when the conscious mind turns away from the subject or problem when the inventor's focus is on something else, or while relaxing or sleeping. A novel idea may come in a flash—a [Eureka](/wiki/Eureka_effect "Eureka effect")! moment. For example, after years of working to figure out the general theory of relativity, the solution came to Einstein suddenly in a dream "like a giant die making an indelible impress, a huge map of the universe outlined itself in one clear vision".[[27]](#cite_note-27) Inventions can also be accidental, such as in the case of [polytetrafluoroethylene](/wiki/Polytetrafluoroethylene "Polytetrafluoroethylene") (Teflon).

#### Insight

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Invention&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Insight")]

Insight can also be a vital element of invention. Such inventive insights may begin with questions, doubt or a [hunch](/wiki/Intuition_(knowledge) "Intuition (knowledge)"). It may begin by recognizing that something unusual or accidental may be useful or that it could open a new avenue for exploration. For example, the odd metallic color of plastic made by accidentally adding a thousand times too much catalyst led scientists to explore its metal-like properties, inventing electrically conductive plastic and light emitting plastic—an invention that won the Nobel Prize in 2000 and has led to innovative lighting, display screens, wallpaper and much more (see [conductive polymer](/wiki/Conductive_polymer "Conductive polymer"), and [organic light-emitting diode](/wiki/Organic_light-emitting_diode "Organic light-emitting diode") or [OLED](/wiki/OLED "OLED")).[[28]](#cite_note-28)

#### Exploration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Invention&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Exploration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Photographing_Sound_in_1884._A_rare_photograph_taken_at_Volta_Laboratory_by_J._Harris_Rogers%2C_a_friend_of_Bell_and_Tainter_%28Smithsonian_photo_44312-E%29_i009.jpg/330px-Photographing_Sound_in_1884._A_rare_photograph_taken_at_Volta_Laboratory_by_J._Harris_Rogers%2C_a_friend_of_Bell_and_Tainter_%28Smithsonian_photo_44312-E%29_i009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Photographing_Sound_in_1884._A_rare_photograph_taken_at_Volta_Laboratory_by_J._Harris_Rogers,_a_friend_of_Bell_and_Tainter_(Smithsonian_photo_44312-E)_i009.jpg)

A rare 1884 photo showing the experimental recording of voice patterns by a photographic process at the [Alexander Graham Bell Laboratory](/wiki/Volta_Laboratory_and_Bureau "Volta Laboratory and Bureau") in Washington, D.C. Many of their experimental designs panned out in failure.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Eric_Magnus_Tigerstedt.jpg/250px-Eric_Magnus_Tigerstedt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eric_Magnus_Tigerstedt.jpg)

[Eric M. C. Tigerstedt](/wiki/Eric_M._C._Tigerstedt "Eric M. C. Tigerstedt") (1887–1925) was known as a pioneer of [sound-on-film](/wiki/Sound_film "Sound film") technology. Tigerstedt in 1915.

Invention is often an exploratory process with an uncertain or unknown outcome. There are failures as well as successes. Inspiration can start the process, but no matter how complete the initial idea, inventions typically must be developed.

#### Improvement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Invention&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Improvement")]

Inventors may, for example, try to improve something by making it more effective, healthier, faster, more efficient, easier to use, serve more purposes, longer lasting, cheaper, more [ecologically](/wiki/Ecological "Ecological") friendly, or [aesthetically](/wiki/Aesthetic "Aesthetic") different, lighter weight, more [ergonomic](/wiki/Ergonomic "Ergonomic"), structurally different, with new light or color properties, etc.

### Implementation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Invention&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Implementation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Arabic_Numerals.svg/250px-Arabic_Numerals.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Arabic_Numerals.svg)

[Western Arabic numerals](/wiki/Western_Arabic_numerals "Western Arabic numerals") — an example of non-material inventions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/ZST_Bratislava_Petrzalka_3.jpg/250px-ZST_Bratislava_Petrzalka_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:ZST_Bratislava_Petrzalka_3.jpg)

Railways — probably the most important invention in [land transport](/wiki/Land_transport "Land transport") (railway station in [Bratislava](/wiki/Bratislava "Bratislava"), Slovakia)

In [economic theory](/wiki/Economic_theory "Economic theory"), inventions are one of the chief examples of "[positive externalities](/wiki/Externality "Externality")", a beneficial side effect that falls on those outside a transaction or activity. One of the central concepts of economics is that externalities should be internalized—unless some of the benefits of this positive externality can be captured by the parties, the parties are under-rewarded for their inventions, and systematic under-rewarding leads to under-investment in activities that lead to inventions. The [patent](/wiki/Patent "Patent") system captures those [positive externalities](/wiki/Externality "Externality") for the inventor or other patent owner so that the economy as a whole invests an optimum amount of resources in the invention process.

## Comparison with innovation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Invention&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Comparison with innovation")]

Main article: [Innovation](/wiki/Innovation "Innovation")

In contrast to invention, innovation is the implementation of a [creative](/wiki/Creativity "Creativity") idea that specifically leads to greater value or usefulness. That is, while an invention may be useless or have no value yet still be an invention, an innovation must have some sort of value, typically economic.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/US_Patent_cover_%28US_Patent_5%2C860%2C492%29.jpg/250px-US_Patent_cover_%28US_Patent_5%2C860%2C492%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Patent_cover_(US_Patent_5,860,492).jpg)

[U.S. patent](/wiki/United_States_patent_law "United States patent law")

The term *invention* is also an important legal concept and central to patent law systems worldwide. As is often the case for legal concepts, its legal meaning is slightly different from common usage of the word. Additionally, the *legal* concept of invention is quite different in American and European patent law.

In Europe, the first test a patent application must pass is, "Is this an invention?" If it is, subsequent questions are whether it is new and sufficiently inventive. The implication—counter-intuitively—is that a legal invention is not inherently novel. Whether a patent application relates to an invention is governed by Article 52 of the European Patent Convention, that excludes, e.g., discoveries *as such* and software *as such*. The EPO Boards of Appeal decided that the technical character of an application is decisive for it to represent an invention, following an age-old Italian and German tradition. British courts do not agree with this interpretation. Following a 1959 Australian decision ("NRDC"), they believe that it is not possible to grasp the invention concept in a single rule. A British court once stated that the technical character test implies a "restatement of the problem in more imprecise terminology."

In the United States, all patent applications are considered inventions. The statute explicitly says that the American invention concept includes discoveries (35 USC § 100(a)), contrary to the European invention concept. The European invention concept corresponds to the American "patentable subject matter" concept: the first test a patent application is submitted to. While the statute (35 USC § 101)[[29]](#cite_note-USPTO-29) virtually poses no limits to patenting whatsoever, courts have decided in binding precedents that abstract ideas, natural phenomena and laws of nature are not patentable. Various attempts have been made to substantiate the "abstract idea" test, which suffers from abstractness itself, but none have succeeded. The last attempt so far was the "machine or transformation" test, but the U.S. Supreme Court decided in 2010 that it is merely an indication at best.

In India, invention means a new product or process that involves an inventive step, and capable of being made or used in an industry. Whereas, "new invention" means any invention that has not been anticipated in any prior art or used in the country or anywhere in the world.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

## In the arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Invention&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: In the arts")]

Invention has a long and important history in [the arts](/wiki/The_arts "The arts"). Inventive thinking has always played a vital role in the [creative process](/wiki/Creative_process "Creative process").[[31]](#cite_note-31) While some inventions in the arts are [patentable](/wiki/Patentable "Patentable"), others are not because they cannot fulfill the strict requirements governments have established for granting them. (see [patent](/wiki/Patent "Patent")).

Some inventions in art include the:

* [Collage](/wiki/Collage "Collage") and construction invented by Picasso
* [Readymade](/wiki/Readymade "Readymade") art invented by [Marcel Duchamp](/wiki/Marcel_Duchamp "Marcel Duchamp")
* [mobile](/wiki/Mobile_(sculpture) "Mobile (sculpture)") invented by [Alexander Calder](/wiki/Alexander_Calder "Alexander Calder")
* Combine invented by [Robert Rauschenberg](/wiki/Robert_Rauschenberg "Robert Rauschenberg")
* Shaped painting invented by [Frank Stella](/wiki/Frank_Stella "Frank Stella")
* [Motion picture](/wiki/Motion_picture "Motion picture"), the invention of which is attributed to [Eadweard Muybridge](/wiki/Eadweard_Muybridge "Eadweard Muybridge")[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)
* Video art invented by [Nam June Paik](/wiki/Nam_June_Paik "Nam June Paik")[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Likewise, [Jackson Pollock](/wiki/Jackson_Pollock "Jackson Pollock") invented an entirely new form of painting and a new kind of abstraction by dripping, pouring, splashing and splattering paint onto un-stretched canvas lying on the floor.

Inventive tools of the artist's trade also produced advances in creativity. [Impressionist](/wiki/Impressionist "Impressionist") painting became possible because of newly invented collapsible, resealable metal paint tubes that facilitated spontaneous painting outdoors.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Inventions originally created in the form of artwork can also develop other uses, e.g. Alexander Calder's mobile, which is now commonly used over babies' cribs. Funds generated from patents on inventions in art, design and architecture can support the realization of the invention or other creative work. [Frédéric Auguste Bartholdi](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Auguste_Bartholdi "Frédéric Auguste Bartholdi")'s 1879 [design patent](/wiki/Design_patent "Design patent") on the [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty") helped fund the famous statue because it covered small replicas, including those sold as souvenirs.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The [timeline for invention in the arts](/wiki/Timeline_for_invention_in_the_arts "Timeline for invention in the arts") lists the most notable artistic inventors.

## Gender gap in inventions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Invention&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Gender gap in inventions")]

Historically, women in many regions have been unrecognised for their inventive contributions (except [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") and [France](/wiki/France "France")[[37]](#cite_note-37)), despite being the sole inventor or co-inventor in inventions, including highly notable inventions. Notable examples include [Margaret Knight](/wiki/Margaret_E._Knight "Margaret E. Knight") who faced significant challenges in receiving credit for her inventions;[[38]](#cite_note-38) [Elizabeth Magie](/wiki/Lizzie_Magie "Lizzie Magie") who was not credited for her invention of the game of *[Monopoly](/wiki/Monopoly_(game) "Monopoly (game)")*;[[39]](#cite_note-39) and among other such examples, [Chien-Shiung Wu](/wiki/Chien-Shiung_Wu "Chien-Shiung Wu") whose male colleagues alone were awarded the Nobel Prize for their joint contributions to physics.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Societal prejudice, institutional, educational and often legal patent barriers have both played a role in the gender invention gap. For example, although there could be found female patenters in US patent Office who also are likely to be helpful in their experience, still a patent applications made to the [US Patent Office](/wiki/United_States_Patent_and_Trademark_Office "United States Patent and Trademark Office") for inventions are less likely to succeed where the applicant have a "feminine" name,[[41]](#cite_note-41) and additionally women could lose their independent legal patent rights to their husbands once married.[[42]](#cite_note-42) See also the [gender gap in patents](/wiki/Patent#Gender_gap_in_patents "Patent").
